pattern: Grouping values by remainder mod k turns the forbidden-difference rule into independent chains that can each be counted with a small Fibonacci-style pick/skip recurrence, then multiplied together.
hints: Two values can only ever conflict if they differ by exactly k, so group values that share the same remainder mod k -- values in different groups never interact.
Sort each group; if you line its distinct values up, only neighbors that are exactly k apart are actually in conflict, forming a chain much like the classic house-robber setup.

For each chain, count subset choices with a pick/skip recurrence that also multiplies in 2^(count-1) ways per repeated value, then multiply the totals across all chains and subtract 1 for the empty subset.

edge_inputs.description: one chain of evenly spaced values
maximum length, all identical values so no two distinct values ever conflict
duplicates within a chain multiply the count rather than just extending it
k larger than every pairwise difference, so nothing ever conflicts
two separate chains that must be counted independently then multiplied
a long single chain exercising the pick/skip recurrence repeatedly

stress.description: near max-length arrays with a small k so many collisions are possible
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 18))
    k = rng.randint(1, 5)
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 10) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums, k]
